summary: Gruber analyzes Spotify's 'Time to Play Fair' complaint against Apple's App Store policies, finding some arguments valid but others disingenuous. He points out that Spotify's comparison to Uber and Deliveroo is misleading since physical goods have never been subject to IAP requirements. However, he agrees that Apple's prohibition on apps even telling users how to subscribe via a web browser is indefensible. He notes Apple's official response is well-crafted but conspicuously avoids addressing this specific anti-steering policy. Gruber argues Spotify would be stronger focusing on the core issues rather than padding their complaint with weaker arguments.
key_points: Spotify's comparison to Uber and Deliveroo is disingenuous — physical goods and services have never been subject to Apple's IAP requirement
Apple hasn't singled out Spotify specifically; the 30% rule applies to all digital content and subscription categories equally
The prohibition on apps even telling users how to subscribe outside the App Store is the strongest and most indefensible part of Apple's policy
Apple's detailed response to Spotify is cogent but conspicuously avoids defending the anti-steering provision
Spotify's complaint is diluted by weaker arguments that distract from the issues where they have the strongest legal footing
money_quotes: I think Spotify (along with any other company selling digital content or subscriptions) has a case. But they're being disingenuous comparing themselves to Uber and Deliveroo.
Apple hasn't singled out Spotify. They've singled out the categories of digital content and subscriptions. They're in the same boat as Netflix.
The 'we can't even tell them that or point them in the right direction' is a sticking point for me.
What Apple should do is allow apps that opt out of IAP to explain that users need to subscribe or make purchases using a web browser, and allow them to link to their website from within the app.
Everything else in Spotify's list of complaints seems like noise to me, and distracts from the central issues — which happen to be the issues where Spotify should be on the strongest legal footing.

Apple conspicuously avoids addressing the fact that apps like Spotify aren't even allowed to tell users how to subscribe using a web browser. Apple executives should take a hard look at why they chose not to defend that policy.
key_insight: Spotify has a legitimate grievance about Apple's anti-steering rules that prevent apps from even telling users how to subscribe outside the App Store, but weakens its case by padding the complaint with disingenuous comparisons to physical goods companies.
